narrative: Dear Maria, imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a collection of numbers, but as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, and unfortunately, the patterns they've seen in thousands of similar stories led them to predict a tough road ahead for passing the bar exam—with about a 58.5% chance of not making it through. At the heart of this was your Year 3 decile of 1, the lowest ranking in your school, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward that risk, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as law school demands ramped up. Your LSAT score of 25.5 echoed this concern as the next biggest factor, placing you below the levels that have historically correlated with bar success for students like you. Even your solid Year 1 decile of 2, while a bright spot early on, couldn't outweigh these, as it ranked third in influence toward the predicted struggle. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as the team knows this bracket often means fewer resources for the intensive prep needed, making it a notable pull in the same direction. Your Hispanic background has shown up in past data as linked to higher hurdles in this high-stakes exam, influencing the outlook further, followed by your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is respectable but didn't tip the scales enough. Being a full-time student and female rounded out the picture, each nudging the prediction toward failure based on broader trends. It's not that any one part of your profile doomed you—it's this combination painting a cautious tale the model couldn't ignore. You've got real potential, especially that early decile showing your grit, and with targeted steps like LSAT retakes or extra bar prep support, you could rewrite this story. We're rooting for you to prove the patterns wrong and shine in law. Keep pushing forward.
